**PAPER**

# Lysozyme-directed synthesis of platinum nanoclusters as a mimic oxidase†

Cheng-Ju Yu,<sup>‡a</sup> Tzu-Heng Chen,<sup>‡a</sup> Jhih-Yu Jiang<sup>a</sup> and Wei-Lung Tseng<sup>*ab</sup>

We present a simple, one-pot approach for synthesizing ultrafine platinum (Pt) nanoclusters (NCs) under alkaline conditions using lysozyme (Lys) as a template. From the analysis of the nanoclusters by matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry, Lys-stabilized Pt NCs majorly consisted of Pt<sub>4</sub> clusters. The formation of Pt NCs was confirmed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy. The maximal fluorescence of Pt NCs appears at 434 nm with a quantum yield of 0.08, a fluorescence lifetime of 3.0 ns, and excitation-dependent emission wavelength behavior. Pt NCs exhibit an intrinsic oxidase-like activity because Pt NCs can catalyze O<sub>2</sub> oxidation of organic substrates through a four-electron reduction process. Compared with larger Pt nanoparticles, the Pt NCs produce substantially greater catalytic activity in the O<sub>2</sub>-mediated oxidation of 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulphonic acid), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine, and dopamine.

## 1 Introduction

Molecule-like metal-based (Au, Ag, and Pt) nanoclusters (NCs), comprising several to tens of atoms, have attracted a considerable amount of attention in the fields of heavy metal and biomolecule sensing, catalysis, biological imaging and labeling, and fuel cells.<sup>1,2</sup> Compared to metal nanoparticles (NPs) with diameters greater than 2 nm, NCs exhibit greater catalytic activities and have discrete electronic structures.<sup>3</sup> A high percentage of the metal content of metal NCs is exposed to the nanoparticle surface. For example, in a 2.0 nm NC, 50% of the metal atoms are exposed. Consequently, metal NCs are highly active catalysts for several crucial reactions; Au NCs have been shown to catalyze the oxidation of carbon monoxide,<sup>4,5</sup> the reduction of 4-nitrophenol,<sup>6</sup> and the reduction of oxygen.<sup>7</sup> Pt NCs exhibit extraordinarily high levels of activity in oxidizing carbon monoxide,<sup>8</sup> reducing oxygen,<sup>9</sup> and oxidatively dehydrogenating propane.<sup>10</sup> Metal NPs, including Pt, Au, and Ag NPs, often exhibit peroxidase- and oxidase-like activities, and can catalyze the H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>-mediated oxidation of peroxidase substrates, the oxidation of glucose with co-substrate O<sub>2</sub>, and the oxidation of 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulphonic acid) (ABTS) and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of O<sub>2</sub>.<sup>11–15</sup> Au NCs exhibit peroxidase-like activity, and can catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>.<sup>16</sup> However, few studies have reported the use of metal NCs to mimic oxidase.

Although these NCs do not possess plasmonic properties, they interact with light and undergo electronic transitions between discrete energy levels. The emission wavelength of metal NCs is tunable from the near-infrared to ultraviolet (UV) regions by changing the number of metal atoms that are present within the clusters.<sup>17</sup> For example, poly(amidoamine) dendrimer-encapsulated Au<sub>5</sub>, Au<sub>8</sub>, Au<sub>13</sub>, Au<sub>23</sub>, and Au<sub>31</sub> clusters produce UV, blue, green, red, and near-IR emissions, respectively.<sup>18</sup> Moreover, Ag NCs with core sizes ranging from Ag<sub>2</sub> to Ag<sub>8</sub> emit over a range of 400 to 600 nm.<sup>19,20</sup> However, in all of the metal NCs, relatively few studies have been devoted to the preparation of fluorescent Pt NCs. Kawasaki *et al.* proposed a one-pot, surfactant-free approach for synthesizing fluorescent Pt NCs with 4 to 6 Pt atoms in a *N,N*-dimethylformamide solution without capping ligands.<sup>21</sup> Poly(amidoamine) dendrimers with terminating hydroxyl groups were used as templates to direct the growth of fluorescent Pt<sub>5</sub> clusters and non-fluorescent Pt NPs by reducing H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> with NaBH<sub>4</sub>.<sup>22</sup> In another approach, a two-step process was developed to synthesize Pt NCs. First, nanometer-sized Pt particles were prepared by reducing H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> with NaBH<sub>4</sub> in the presence of a capping agent, mercaptosuccinic acid.<sup>23</sup> The formed Pt particles were then etched to form Pt NCs by ligand exchange between the capping ligand and glutathione (GSH) on the Pt surface. However, these methods typically have long reaction times, produce bulky Pt NPs, require the use of toxic organic solvents, and require multiple steps.

Lysozyme (Lys) can act as both a reducing and stabilizing agent in producing a wide variety of NPs, including Au,<sup>24</sup> Ag,<sup>25</sup> Si,<sup>26</sup> and TiO<sub>2</sub>-based NPs.<sup>26</sup> Recently, Lys was successfully used as a template for synthesizing Au NCs. The resulting Lys-stabilized Au NCs were shown to inhibit bacterial growth,<sup>27</sup> detect Hg(II) and CH<sub>3</sub>Hg(I),<sup>28</sup> sense GSH,<sup>29</sup> and catalyze H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>-mediated oxidation of TMB in the presence of graphene.<sup>30</sup> We report a one-pot synthesis of fluorescent Pt NCs through the reduction of H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> with Lys at a low temperature (37 °C). The as-prepared fluorescent Pt NCs produce an intense blue emission at 437 nm, and in the presence of dissolved O<sub>2</sub>, exhibit oxidase-like activity to oxidize ABTS, TMB, and dopamine.

## 2 Materials and methods

### Materials

Lys (chicken egg white) was purchased from MP Biomedicals (Irvine, CA). H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub>·6H<sub>2</sub>O, FeCl<sub>3</sub>·6H<sub>2</sub>O, FeCl<sub>2</sub>·4H<sub>2</sub>O, HAuCl<sub>4</sub>·3H<sub>2</sub>O, NaBH<sub>4</sub>, Na<sub>2</sub>HPO<sub>4</sub>, NaH<sub>2</sub>PO<sub>4</sub>, NaCl, dopamine, ABTS, TMB, methylene blue, sodium acetate, sodium citrate, catalase (9473 units per mg, from *Aspergillus niger*), horseradish peroxidase (HRP; 50–150 units per mg), and α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid were ordered from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Milli-Q ultrapure water (Milli-pore, Hamburg, Germany) was used in all of the experiments.

### Characterization of Pt NCs

Absorption, fluorescence, and Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra of the Pt NCs were recorded using a double-beam UV-visible spectrometer (Cintra 10e, GBC, Victoria, Australia), a Hitachi F-7000 fluorometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), and a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo electron Corporation, Madison, WI), respectively. Time-resolved fluorescence measurements were conducted using an OB920 single-photon counting fluorometer (Edinburgh Analytical Instruments) with a pulsed nanosecond nitrogen lamp as the excitation source. The elemental compositions of the Pt NCs were obtained using JAMP-9500F auger electron spectroscopy (JEOL, Japan). Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS) (Bruker Daltonics, Germany) was performed to determine the molecular weight of the Pt NCs. Prior to MALDI-TOF MS measurement, we mixed saturated α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid containing 70% acetonitrile with an equal volume of the purified Pt NCs and pipetted the resulting mixture onto a stainless steel 384-well target (Bruker Daltonics, Germany). In positive ion and reflection mode, each final mass spectrum was accumulated as an average of 2000 single shot spectra. Laser fluence was set to 72 μJ per 100 μm<sup>2</sup>. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-1400; JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) was used to measure the size and composition of the Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>, Pt, and Au NPs.

### Preparation of Pt NCs, Pt NPs, Au NPs, and Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> NPs

The preparation of the Pt NCs was performed by chemical reduction of H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> with Lys, as a soft template. A solution of Lys (5 mL, 1–25 mg mL<sup>−1</sup>) reacted with an equal volume of 10 mM H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub>. The mixture was vigorously stirred for 2 min, followed by the rapid addition of NaOH (500 μL, 1 M). The obtained solution was incubated at 37 °C overnight. To remove excess Lys, a solution of Pt NCs was dialysed with 5 mM phosphate buffer (pH 3) for two days. The concentration of the obtained Pt NCs is denoted as 1×. The preparation of Pt NPs (5 ± 2 nm and 30 ± 4 nm), Au NPs (13 ± 2 nm), and Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> NPs (13 ± 2 nm) is described in the ESI.†

### Pt NC-induced oxidation reaction

Stock solutions of ABTS, TMB, and dopamine were prepared in deionized water. Pt NCs (50 μL, 1×) were added to a solution consisting of ABTS (100 μL, 1 mM), deionized water (350 μL), and acetate (500 μL, 100 mM; pH 2.0–6.0). The pH of acetate solution was adjusted by adding NaOH or HCl. The mixture was incubated at ambient temperature for 0–60 min. The absorption spectra of the mixture were recorded using a visible spectrophotometer. Under the same reaction conditions, TMB and dopamine were used instead of ABTS, once at a time. To study the steady-state mechanism of the Pt NC-induced oxidation reaction, variable concentrations of TMB (0.1–1 mM) was used as substrates while the concentration of the Pt NCs (0.05×) was kept constant. The zero order rate constants were obtained by linear plotting of the maximal absorbance *versus* time. Steady-state kinetic assays were carried out in 50 mM sodium acetate (pH 3.0). The kinetic parameters, $V_{max}$ and $K_m$, were calculated according to the Michaelis–Menten equation.

## 3 Results and discussion

### Synthesis and characterization of Pt NCs

The preparation of Pt NCs was done by reducing H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> with Lys under basic conditions at 37 °C for 12 h. When the concentration of Lys varied from 1 to 25 mg mL<sup>−1</sup> at a fixed concentration of H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> (10 mM), the reaction of H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> with 5 mg mL<sup>−1</sup> (approximately 0.35 mM) Lys showed the most intense fluorescence intensity at 434 nm (Fig. S1, ESI†). At the Lys concentrations of 1 and 2 mg mL<sup>−1</sup>, the formed Pt NCs exhibited weaker fluorescence as a result of the production of large-sized Pt NCs. Similar phenomena were observed when bovine serum albumin (BSA) and Lys were used to prepare BSA- and Lys-stabilized AuNCs, respectively.<sup>28,31</sup> In the concentration range of 10–25 mg mL<sup>−1</sup> Lys, the fluorescence of the as-prepared Pt NCs progressively decreased with increasing Lys concentrations. This is probably due to the fact that a large amount of the precursor was consumed in the formation of nuclei, resulting in a decrease in the formation of Pt NCs. We suggest that the role of Lys is to act as the sole reducing agent. Thus, a high molar ratio of Lys to H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> facilitates the formation of Pt NCs, while a low molar ratio of Lys to H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> induces the production of Pt NPs and Pt NCs. Taken together, 5 mg mL<sup>−1</sup> was selected for the subsequent study. Because the size of the resulting Pt NCs was too small to be observed in standard TEM,<sup>1</sup> MALDI-TOF MS was used to determine the size of the metal NCs<sup>1</sup> and confirm the production of Pt NCs. The molecular weight of Lys was determined to be 14.3 kDa by using MADLI-TOF MS (Fig. 1A). When Lys was incubated with a solution of H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub>, the resulting solution was immediately detected using MALDI-TOF-MS. Fig. S2 (ESI†) shows that the formation of Au<sup>+</sup>–Lys complexes caused a shift in molecular mass. Compared to Lys and Au<sup>+</sup>–Lys complexes, the MALDI-TOF MS spectrum for Lys-stabilized Pt NCs consisted of several peaks between m/z 14 000 and 16 000 (Fig. 1B). One of these peaks appearing at 14 318 was assigned to [Lys + H]<sup>+</sup>, while other peaks with an average m/z of 15 098 corresponded to different sizes of Lys-stabilized Pt NCs. According to the difference in the m/z value between the above average peak and the Lys peak, the average number of Pt atoms in Lys-stabilized Pt NCs is 4. This signifies that Pt<sub>4</sub> clusters are major products in a mixture of Pt NCs. This result also reflects that Lys may remain bound to Pt NCs during the MALDI-TOF MS process. Similar phenomena occurred during the MALDI-TOF MS analysis of protein-stabilized Au<sub>8</sub> and Au<sub>25</sub> clusters.<sup>29,31,32</sup> Differences in the molecular weights of Lys and Lys-stabilized Pt provide clear evidence for the formation of Pt NCs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to determine the valence state of Pt NCs. The XPS spectrum of dried Pt NCs revealed a peak indicative of Pt 4f<sub>7/2</sub> at 71.7 eV, which differs from the reported XPS peaks for PtCl<sub>4</sub>, PtCl<sub>2</sub>, and bulk Pt, which appear at 75.0, 73.2, and 70.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 2).<sup>9</sup>

![Fig. 1 MALDI-TOF mass spectra of (A) Lys and (B) Lys-stabilized Pt NCs.](figure1.png)

![Fig. 2 X-ray photoelectron spectrum of Lys-stabilized Pt NCs. The original spectrum is in black, the fitted spectrum is in red, the Pt(I) 4f<sub>7/2</sub> spectrum is in blue, and the Pt(0) 4f<sub>7/2</sub> spectrum is in green.](figure2.png)

Moreover, the peak position of Pt 4f<sub>7/2</sub> for Pt NCs appears between the Pt(0) and Pt(II) peak positions. Relative to the binding energy of bulk Pt, Pt NCs showed the positive shift in the Pt 4f<sub>7/2</sub> peak position. This shift is attributed to the fact that the binding energy of metal clusters increases with reducing cluster size. The best fit of the data reveals that the Pt NCs contained approximately 67% Pt(I) and 33% Pt(0); there was no evidence for the presence of an oxidized precursor, Pt(II)Cl<sub>4</sub><sup>2−</sup>. This is similar to the reported composition of glutathione-protected Pt NCs, which contained 90% Pt(I) and a complementary Pt(0) content.<sup>23</sup> Lin and Tseng reported that Hg<sup>2+</sup> induces fluorescence quenching of Au<sub>25</sub> clusters through the formation of metallophilic bonds between Hg(II) (4f<sup>14</sup>5d<sup>10</sup>) and Au(I) (4f<sup>14</sup>5d<sup>10</sup>).<sup>28</sup> This effect could be applied to demonstrate the presence of Pt(0) in Pt NCs because Pt(0) (4f<sup>14</sup>5d<sup>10</sup>) can bind to Hg(II) through metallophilic interactions.<sup>33</sup> Fig. S3 (ESI†) shows that fluorescence quenching of Pt NCs occurs when the Hg(II) ion is added, confirming the presence of Pt(0) in Pt NCs. FT-IR was used to examine the interactions between Lys and Pt NCs. The FT-IR spectrum of Lys contains two major bands at 1664 and 1543 cm<sup>−1</sup>, which correspond to amide I (C=O stretching) and amide II (N–H bending coupled with C–N stretching) bonds, respectively (curve a in Fig. S4, ESI†). By contrast, the FT-IR spectrum of Lys-stabilized Pt NCs shows less intense absorptions at 1664 and 1543 cm<sup>−1</sup>, suggesting that there are fewer amide I and II bonds present because the occurrence of the Lys secondary structure decreased (curve b in Fig. S4, ESI†). Comparing the Lys and Lys-stabilized Pt NC spectra reveals that the Lys amide I and II bonds are blue-shifted in the complex, suggesting that Pt NCs interact with the Lys peptide bonds. Sennuga *et al.* reported that the interaction between Pt NPs and apoferritin results in similar shifts in the amide I and II absorption bands.<sup>34</sup>

### Optical properties and stability of Pt NCs

In a solution, H<sub>2</sub>PtCl<sub>6</sub> has an absorbance maximum at 260 nm (Fig 3A), which results from a ligand-to-metal charge transfer within the [PtCl<sub>6</sub>]<sup>2−</sup> ions.<sup>35</sup> Following the addition of Lys to a solution of the [PtCl<sub>6</sub>]<sup>2−</sup> ions, the absorbance intensity at 260 nm decreases (Fig. 3A), whereas the fluorescence gradually increases (Fig. 3B). After incubating overnight at 37 °C, the fluorescence intensity of a mixture of the [PtCl<sub>6</sub>]<sup>2−</sup> complex ions and Lys was remarkably larger than that of Lys (Fig. S5, ESI†). These results suggest that the observed blue emission arises from the Pt NCs, and not from Lys. Thus, a mixture of the [PtCl<sub>6</sub>]<sup>2−</sup> complex ions and Lys progressively converted to Pt NCs over time. The maximal emission shifts to longer wavelengths when the excitation wavelength increases from 350 to 500 nm, suggesting that the as-prepared solution contains a mixture of Pt NCs with different particle sizes (Fig. 3C). When a long pass filter (475 nm cut-off filter) was used to block scattering from the excitation wavelength, we still observed that the maximal emission shifted from 510 to 556 nm by changing the excitation wavelength from 425 to 465 nm (Fig. S6, ESI†). We rule out that excitation-dependent change in spectra is due to scattering from Lys and Pt NCs. Thus, excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior arises from different sized NCs in the as-prepared solution. Kawasaki *et al.* found that the maximal fluorescence emission wavelength for ligand-free Pt NCs increases when the excitation wavelength increases.<sup>21</sup> The most intense fluorescence emission occurs at 434 nm when the solution was excited at 370 nm, indicating that Pt<sub>4</sub> clusters are dominant in a mixture of Pt NCs, according to the spherical Jellium model:<sup>36</sup>

$$E_{Fermi}/N^{1/3} \text{ (} E_{Fermi} = 4.28 \text{ eV for Pt).}$$

![Fig. 3 (A) Absorption and (B) fluorescence spectra obtained from the synthesis of Pt NCs at different growth times. (C) Fluorescence spectra of Pt NCs with excitation of different wavelengths. (D) Fluorescence lifetime decay of Pt NCs.](figure3.png)

A tetrahedral geometry provides the lowest energy structure for Pt<sub>4</sub> clusters.<sup>37</sup> Using quinine sulfate as the reference, we estimated that the Pt NCs provide a quantum yield of 8% (Fig. S7, ESI†). Numerically fitting the fluorescence at 434 nm (excitation at 370 nm) revealed a fluorescence lifetime of 3.0 ns for the blue-emitting Pt NCs (Fig. 3D). This short lifetime may originate from singlet transitions between the filled d- and sp-conduction bands. To demonstrate the formation of different sized Pt NCs, lifetime values of green-emitting Pt NCs were obtained by numerically fitting the fluorescence at 520 nm (excitation at 485 nm). Fig. S8 (ESI†) shows that Pt NCs exhibited two lifetimes of 5.4 (73%) and 36.1 ns (27%). We tested the stability of Pt NCs by monitoring their fluorescence in a 50 mM phosphate solution (pH 7.0) containing 0–400 mM NaCl (Fig. S9, ESI†). The fluorescence remained constant for the range of test conditions, indicating that Pt NCs have potential for molecular sensing under physiological conditions.

### Pt NCs mimic oxidase activity

HRP catalyzes the H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>-mediated oxidation of ABTS and TMB. These dyes have applications in bioassay, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), and biosensing applications. Recently, NP-based materials, including Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> and Au NPs, were used in place of HRP because they offer greater resistance to denaturation and protease digestion and are simpler to prepare and store.<sup>11,38,39</sup> However, the peroxidase-catalyzed reactions require H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> as an electron acceptor. CeO<sub>2</sub> NPs, Pt nanodot-coated Au nanorods, and PdPt alloy nanodot-coated Au nanorods exhibit oxidase-like activity in the catalytic O<sub>2</sub> oxidation of ABTS and TMB.<sup>40–42</sup> Because the template-based direct synthesis of metal NCs does not require the use of stabilizing ligands, metal clusters located in templates can provide non-block, active surfaces for catalyzing chemical reactions.<sup>1</sup> We used Lys as a template for synthesizing Pt NCs and investigated whether Lys-stabilized Pt NCs catalyze the oxidation of ABTS and TMB. Lys-stabilized Pt NCs were incubated with ABTS in 50 mM acetate solution at pH 2.0–6.0. Fig. 4A and B show the time-dependent absorbance at 414 nm and color changes of 0.1 mM ABTS following the addition of 0.05× Lys-stabilized Pt NCs to an ABTS solution with a pH ranging from 2.0 to 6.0. Fig. S10 (ESI†) shows their corresponding visible spectra. In the absence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, the maximal catalytic rate for Pt NC-catalyzed oxidation of ABTS was at pH 2.0. In the absence of Pt NCs, no oxidation of ABTS is apparent over the pH range of 2.0 to 6.0 (Fig. S11, ESI†). Under the same concentration of Pt NCs, 0.1 mM TMB underwent Pt NC-induced oxidation and color change at acidic pH levels (Fig. 4B and S12, ESI†). By monitoring the change in absorbance at 650 nm, the maximal catalytic rate for Pt NC-catalyzed one electron oxidation of TMB was at pH 3.0. This result is inconsistent with our prediction that the most rapid oxidation of TMB occurs at pH 2.0 in the presence of Pt NCs. This is probably attributed to the fact that Pt NCs catalyze two electron oxidation of TMB at pH 2.0, generating the oxidized TMB with an absorbance maximum at 450 nm.<sup>43</sup> We used dopamine to verify the Pt NCs' oxidation properties because of its resistance to oxidation. Adding 0.05× Pt NCs to a solution of 0.1 mM dopamine at pH 7.0 caused the solution to change from colorless to orange (Fig. 4B) and the characteristic absorption bands in the UV-visible spectrum to emerge (Fig. S13, ESI†). These changes are characteristic of dopamine conversion to aminochrome, a major oxidation product.<sup>44</sup> In the absence of Pt NCs, the UV-visible spectrum and color of the dopamine solution remain almost constant, even after days of incubation. Taken together, these results clearly demonstrate the intrinsic oxidase-like activity of Pt NCs. Fig. 4C shows that, at a pH of 4.0, the catalytic activity of Pt NCs toward the oxidation of TMB follows a Michaelis–Menten mechanism. The Michaelis constant ($K_m$) and maximal velocity ($V_{max}$) were determined to be 0.63 mM and 2.7 μM s<sup>−1</sup>, respectively, by using the Michaelis–Menten equation. Compared to HRP- and Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> NP-catalyzed oxidation of TMB in the presence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>,<sup>38</sup> Pt NCs provide a relatively small value of $K_m$ (high affinity for the substrate), and a relatively large value for $V_{max}$. This result clearly indicates that Pt<sub>4</sub> clusters provide higher catalyst efficiency even in the absence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. Additionally, in comparison with oxidase-like activity of CeO<sub>2</sub> NPs (size ≈ 5–100 nm, $K_m$ = 0.8–3.8 mM, $V_{max}$ = 0.3–0.7 μM s<sup>−1</sup>),<sup>40</sup> Pt NCs show a lower $K_m$ value and a higher $V_{max}$ value than CeO<sub>2</sub> NPs. In addition to examining Pt NCs, we investigated whether HRP, Lys, Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> NPs (13 ± 2 nm), Au NPs (13 ± 2 nm), Pt NPs (30 ± 4 nm), and Pt NPs (5 ± 1 nm) catalyze the oxidation of ABTS in the absence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. However, Fig. 4D reveals that only Pt NCs exhibited high performance in the catalytic oxidation of ABTS.

Fig. 4 (A) Effect of solution pH on the catalytic rate of Pt NCs. 0.05× Pt NCs were incubated with a solution containing 0.1 mM ABTS and 50 mM sodium acetate. (B) Photographs of the colored product obtained from the reaction of 0.05× Pt NCs with 0.1 mM ABTS, 0.1 mM TMB, and 0.1 mM dopamine at pH 2.0, 3.0, and 7.0, respectively. (C) Double reciprocal plot of activity of Pt NCs with the constant concentration of Pt NCs. The velocity ($v$) obtained from the incubation of Pt NCs with different concentrations of TMB at pH 4.0. (D) The intensity of the absorbance at 414 nm of oxidized ABTS obtained in the absence (a) and presence of (b) Lys, (c) HRP, (d) 13 nm diameter Au NPs, (e) 13 nm diameter Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> NPs, (f) 30 nm diameter Pt NPs, (g) 5 nm diameter Pt NPs, and (h) Pt NCs. ABTS (0.1 mM, pH 2.0) was incubated with nanomaterial/protein at ambient temperature for 10 min. The concentration of each nanomaterial and protein was fixed at 5 mg mL<sup>−1</sup>.

The catalytic oxidation of ABTS by Pt-based nanomaterials follows the trend Pt NCs > Pt NPs (5 nm) > Pt NPs (30 nm), suggesting that the catalytic activity of Pt-based nanomaterials is highly dependent on the particle size. This is attributed to the fact that Pt NCs have a high surface area to volume ratio, thus providing more active sites for the catalysis of ABTS. This result also suggests that the use of a template did not block the active surfaces of metal clusters during catalysis. Previous studies have demonstrated that Pt NCs can efficiently catalyze the reduction of oxygen to water through a four-electron reduction reaction in electrocatalytic processes.<sup>9,45</sup> He *et al.* coated Au nanorods with Pt nanodots and used them as oxidase mimetics for the catalytic oxidation of TMB through a four-electron process.<sup>41</sup> They suggested that Au–Pt nanostructures, dissolved O<sub>2</sub>, and TMB served as a catalyst, an electron acceptor, and an electron donor, respectively. Fig. 5 shows that the as-prepared Pt NCs cannot catalyze the oxidation of ABTS under N<sub>2</sub>-saturated conditions, which indicates the importance of O<sub>2</sub> in the oxidation process. Because Pt NCs could reduce O<sub>2</sub> to H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> through a two-electron reduction process [O<sub>2</sub>(g) + 2H<sup>+</sup>(aq) + 2e<sup>−</sup> → H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>(aq)] and then catalyze H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>-mediated oxidation of the organic substrate, catalase converting H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> into water was used to exclude this possibility. Because the absorption spectrum of oxidized ABTS overlaps that of catalase, TMB was used instead of ABTS. Fig. S14 (ESI†) shows that catalase rarely inhibited the Pt NC-catalyzed O<sub>2</sub> oxidation of TMB. According to previous studies and our present findings, we propose that the possible mechanism of Pt NC-catalyzed O<sub>2</sub> oxidation of organic substrates involves a four-electron reduction process [O<sub>2</sub>(g) + 4H<sup>+</sup>(aq) + 4e<sup>−</sup> → 2H<sub>2</sub>O(l)]. Moreover, dissolved O<sub>2</sub> and ABTS act as an electron acceptor and an electron donor, respectively. These findings motivated us to apply Pt NCs to the oxidation of pollutants in environmental water. Following the addition of Pt NCs, the 665 nm maximal absorbance of methylene blue in lake water samples gradually decreased (Fig. S15, ESI†). Pt NCs degrade methylene blue in a complex matrix in the absence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. By contrast, previous studies have reported that high concentrations of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> are required for metal NP-induced degradation of environmental pollutants.<sup>46</sup>

### Conclusions
This study has reported a straightforward one-pot approach to prepare blue-emitting and oxidase-like Pt NCs using Lys as a template at a pH of 12.0. This result is helpful to realize that protein can act as unique template for synthesizing not only Au and Ag NCs, but also Pt NCs. Lys-stabilized Pt NCs catalyze the O<sub>2</sub> oxidation of ABTS, TMB, and dopamine through a four-electron reduction process. We demonstrated the practical applicability of Lys-stabilized Pt NCs in degrading methylene blue in the absence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. We found that the fluorescence quenching of Pt NCs occurs in the presence of Hg(II) and Cu(II), and this is a subject requiring further investigation.

1.  **Experiment Identification**: The text explicitly reports kinetic parameters ($K_m$ and $V_{max}$) in Section 3 ("Results and discussion"), specifically in the paragraph discussing Fig. 4C. The text states: "The Michaelis constant ($K_m$) and maximal velocity ($V_{max}$) were determined to be 0.63 mM and 2.7 μM s<sup>−1</sup>, respectively, by using the Michaelis–Menten equation." This confirms a true kinetic measurement.
2.  **Activity Determination**: The reaction involves the oxidation of TMB by dissolved $O_2$ without added $H_2O_2$, described as "intrinsic oxidase-like activity" and "O<sub>2</sub>-mediated oxidation". Thus, the activity is "oxidase".
3.  **Material Composition**: The nanozyme is identified as "Lys-stabilized Pt NCs" consisting majorly of "Pt<sub>4</sub> clusters". The inorganic formula is "Pt". The surface component is "Lysozyme" (Lys), which acts as the template and stabilizer in the final material.
4.  **Kinetic Parameters**: $K_m$ = "0.63", unit = "mM". $V_{max}$ = "2.7", unit = "μM s<sup>−1</sup>".
5.  **Experimental Conditions**:
    *   **pH**: The kinetic assay was performed at "pH 4.0" (explicitly stated in the sentence describing the determination of $K_m$ and $V_{max}$).
    *   **Catalyst Concentration**: The text states "constant concentration of Pt NCs" and earlier in the methods/figure caption specifies "0.05×". The "1×" concentration is defined as the concentration of the obtained Pt NCs after dialysis, but the exact molar or mass concentration of the "1×" stock is not explicitly defined as a number (e.g., mg/mL or M) in the text, only the dilution factor "0.05×" is given for the assay. Therefore, `ccat_value` is "0.05×" and `ccat_unit` is null (as "×" is a dilution factor, not a standard concentration unit, and the base unit is undefined).
    *   **Substrate Range**: The text mentions "variable concentrations of TMB (0.1–1 mM)". Thus, `c_min` = "0.1", `c_max` = "1", `c_min_unit` = "mM", `c_max_unit` = "mM".
    *   **Temperature**: The text mentions "ambient temperature" for the general reaction but does not provide a specific numerical value (e.g., 25 °C) for the kinetic assay. Thus, `temperature` is null.
    *   **Size**: The text states the size was "too small to be observed in standard TEM" and determined via MALDI-TOF to be Pt4 clusters. No specific length/width/depth values in nm are reported for the final particle dimensions. Thus, size fields are null.
    *   **Reaction Type**: Substrate is TMB. Co-substrate is $O_2$ (dissolved), but the protocol excludes gases. Thus, reaction type is "TMB".
6.  **Exclusion**: No other experiments with explicit $K_m$/$V_{max}$ values are reported.

Simple and Sensitive Point-of-Care Bioassay System Based on Hierarchically Structured Enzyme-Mimetic Nanoparticles Miju Kim, Min Su Kim, Soon Ho Kweon, Sanha Jeong, Mi Hyun Kang, Moon Il Kim, Jinwoo Lee,* and Junsang Doh* Dr. M. Kim, Prof. J. Doh Department of Mechanical Engineering Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH) Pohang, Gyeongbuk 790-784, Republic of Korea E-mail: jsdoh@postech.ac.kr Dr. M. Kim, S. H. Kweon, Prof. J. Doh School of Interdisciplinary Bioscience and Bioengineering (I-Bio) POSTECH Pohang, Gyeongbuk 790-784, Republic of Korea M. S. Kim, S. Jeong, Prof. J. Lee Department of Chemical Engineering POSTECH Pohang, Gyeongbuk 790-784, Republic of Korea E-mail: jinwoo03@postech.ac.kr M. H. Kang Department of Nano Medical Engineering Pusan National University Busan 609-735, Republic of Korea Prof. M. I. Kim Department of BioNano Technology Gachon University Seongnam, Gyeonggi 461-701, Republic of Korea DOI: 10.1002/adhm.201500173 Recently, there are intense interests in the use of nanomaterials for a wide range of biomedical and biotechnological applications.[1] Among known nanomaterials, nanomaterials exhibiting inherent enzyme-like properties have been active area of research over the last decade.[2] Considering extraordinary stability of enzyme-mimetic nanomaterials under harsh environments such as high temperature and extreme pH compared with native enzymes,[3] point-of-care (POC) bioassays would be one of the most promising applications of enzyme-mimetic nanomaterials. Thus, enzyme-mimetic nanomaterial-based bioassay systems have been recently developed for ultrafast and ultrasensitive detection of analytes.[4] However, they require specialized equipments and complicated operations that will limit their applications. Integration of enzyme-mimetic nanomaterials into paper-based disposable platforms that are portable and easy-to-use would resolve such issues and enable enzyme-mimetic nanomaterials to be more widely used for POC applications. Lateral flow immunoassay (LFIA) strips (**Figure 1a**) are one of the most widely used forms of paper-based POC bioassay devices for many applications including pregnancy tests, cardiac marker tests, food and environmental monitoring and defense against bioterrorism.[5] Liquid sample applied on a sample pad of a LIFA strip flows by capillary force to a conjugation pad loaded with nanoparticles (NPs, typically dark-colored NPs such as gold or carbon) conjugated with detection antibodies (Ab–NPs, Figure 1ai). In a conjugation pad, binding of target analytes in sample liquid to the Ab–NPs occurs to form analyte/Ab–NP complexes (Figure 1aii). On a test pad, analyte/Ab–NP complexes will be captured by antibody immobilized on a test line (T-line) by forming sandwich-like structures (Figure 1aiii) while the rest of Ab–NPs will be captured by antibody immobilized on a control line (C-line, Figure 1aiv). As a result, two lines (both T-line and C-line) will be visible by the naked eye if the concentration of target analytes is sufficiently high; otherwise, only one line (C-line) will by visible. It is extremely simple to use LFIA strips; the presence or absence of a target analyte can be visually inspected within 5–20 min after a drop of sample is dispensed onto a sample pad. However, applications of LFIA have been limited due to its low sensitivity.[5b,6] Sensitivity has been increased using various signal-enhancement methods based on fluorescence,[7] chemiluminescence,[8] and heat generation by plasmonic nanoparticles;[9] but the modified LFIAs require specialized devices to detect the signal, so their POC applications are also limited. To overcome these limitations, we integrated enzyme-mimetic nanoparticles into LFIA strips and developed a simple and sensitive bioassay system (**Figure 1b**). The enzyme-mimetic NPs use a peroxidase-like reaction to amplify signals by generating colors on the lines of LFIA strips (Step 2 in Figure 1b), thereby achieve much higher sensitivity than conventional LFIA and make the signal detectable by the naked eye. Furthermore, quantitative information of the analytes can be readily extracted from digital images of LFIA strips acquired by a smart cellular phone, which is being ubiquitously used in the world. To be integrated into LFIA strips, the diameter of NPs should be small enough (typically less than 40 nm[5b]) to efficiently migrate through the porous cellulose nitrate membranes of LFIA strips. In addition, for maximum enzyme-like activity, the total surface area of NPs must be maximized. To satisfy these criteria, Hierarchically structured Pt nanoparticles (H-Pt NPs) with 30-nm diameter and large surface area were synthesized using a seed-mediated growth method[10] in aqueous solution and characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), a scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). The XRD pattern (**Figure 2a**) of the synthesized H-Pt NPs had diffraction peaks at 2θ = 39.3, 45.7, 66.6, and 80.1, which were assigned to the reflections of (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of platinum (JCPDS, no. 88–2343), respectively. Based on the broadening of the XRD peaks, the average crystallite size calculated using the Debye–Scherrer equation was 6.24 nm. The (111)/(200) peak ratio of the Pt NPs was 1.5 times higher than that of the bulk Pt structure, meaning H-Pt NPs exhibited (111)-oriented growth.[11] The synthesized H-Pt NPs were monodispersed (**Figure 2b,c**) with a diameter of ≈30 nm (**Figure 2c**), and had rough, porous surfaces (**Figure 2d**) with 0.212 nm spacing between fringes, which is consistent with the presence of (111) planes (inset, Figure 2d). Considering rough and porous surfaces, the 30 nm H-Pt NPs may be aggregates of smaller Pt NP crystallites. Indeed, the crystallite size of H-Pt NPs calculated from the XRD peaks (6.24 nm) was about 5 times smaller than the diameter of H-Pt NPs measured from the HR-TEM images (30 nm), further supporting our hypothesis. Enzymatic activity of H-Pt NPs was compared with horseradish peroxidase (HRP), an enzyme that is widely used in immunoassays for signal amplification, by performing the peroxidase-catalyzed 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) oxidation reaction in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Figure S1 (Supporting Information), the solution was much deeper blue after reaction with H-Pt NPs than with HRP when the concentration of NPs and HRP were the same ($5 \times 10^{-11}$ M); therefore H-Pt NPs have significantly higher catalytic activity than does HRP. To quantitatively assess peroxidase-like activity of H-Pt NPs, the initial reaction rate of TMB oxidation reaction mediated by H-Pt NPs was measured with various [TMB], where [·] indicates “concentration of”. The kinetic data were described well by the Michaelis–Menten equation (Figure S2, Supporting Information). Kinetic parameters of H-Pt NPs were compared with those of HRP and commercially-available Pt NPs measured in our previous study[12] (**Table 1**). $K_{m}$ of H-Pt NPs was ≈2 times higher than that of HRP; this means that TMB binding affinity of H-Pt NPs is half that of HRP. However, $k_{cat}$ value of H-Pt NPs was ≈14 times higher than that of HRP; this means that H-Pt NPs convert TMB ≈14 times faster than does HRP. Therefore, in the presence of sufficient amounts of substrate, the catalytic reaction of H-Pt NPs would be an order of magnitude faster than that of HRP. Fast reaction rate is essential for urgent POC diagnosis applications. Furthermore, the H-Pt NPs exhibited ≈50 times higher catalytic efficiency per particle ($k_{cat}/K_{m}$) than commercially-available Pt NPs (10 nm) used in our previous study.[4] Catalytic efficiency per mass of H-Pt NPs was also twice as high as that of the commercially-available Pt NPs, possibly due to the rough and porous surfaces of H-Pt NPs. Because H-Pt NPs are inorganic, they are likely to be more stable than HRP in harsh environments. To test this possibility, H-Pt NPs and HRP were incubated at a wide range of temperatures ($T = 4, 15, 22, 34, 45, 60, and 75 °C$) and pHs (2–12) for 3 h, then their catalytic activities were measured using a colorimetric TMB oxidation reaction. Relative activities of H-Pt NPs or HRP treated with various conditions with respect to untreated H-Pt NPs or HRP were calculated and plotted in **Figure 2e,f**. The H-Pt NPs retained good catalytic activity over the entire ranges of $T$ (**Figure 2e**) and pH (**Figure 2f**), whereas that of HRP decreased significantly at $T > 45 °C$, and at pH < 6 or pH > 12. Taken together, the evidence indicates that H-Pt NPs had higher enzymatic activity and better stability than did HRP. With these promising properties, next H-Pt NPs were integrated into LFIA strips (**Figure 1b**). Commercially-available LFIA strips for the detection of human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG), which are available in pharmacy for pregnancy tests, were modified and used. First, anti-hCG antibodies (Abs) were attached on the surfaces of H-Pt NPs to form Ab-H-Pt NPs. Then, conjugation pads of the conventional LFIA, which contains Au NPs conjugated with anti-hCG Abs, were replaced with conjugation pads loaded with Ab-H-Pt NPs. In this way, performance of H-Pt NPs for sensitivity enhancement of LFIAs can be easily assessed without fabricating entire LFIA strips, and sensitivity of H-Pt NP-based LFIAs can be directly compared with that of commercially-available Au NP-based LFIA chips. Two-step sandwich immunoassays (**Figure 1b**) were performed using Ab-H-Pt NP-loaded LFIA chips. In Step 1, a small droplet of sample liquid containing hCG was applied to the sample pad. The sample liquid flows through the conjugation pad containing Ab-H-Pt NPs; during this process, hCG binds to Ab-H-Pt NPs. The hCG/Ab-H-Pt NPs complex is immobilized in the T-line by antibodies that capture hCG, whereas free Ab-H-Pt NPs is immobilized in the C-line by antibodies that bind to anti-hCG antibodies immobilized on Ab-HPt NPs. In Step 2, a small droplet containing H2O2 and 3-amino-9-ethylcarbazole (AEC), another substrate that generates red precipitate when oxidized by peroxidases, was added to the test pads and allowed to react for a defined duration. Finally, images of strips were acquired using a smart cellular phone and quantitative information was extracted by image processing (Figure S3, Supporting Information). Representative digital images of LFIA strips before and 5 min after the addition of AEC were shown in **Figure 3a**. The grey-colored T-line and C-line were each converted to a red line, confirming that successful enzymatic reaction had occurred on the strips. In addition to the color change, the intensity of T-line was also ≈3 times increased in this particular condition ([hCG] = 10 ng mL−1, [AEC] = $3800 \times 10^{-6}$ M) further confirming signal amplification by enzyme-like reaction. To assess enzymatic performance of H-Pt NPs on the strip, kinetic assays were performed on the LFIA strips. First, 10 ng mL−1 of hCG was added to sample pads, then various [AEC] were dispensed onto test pads. Digital images of the test pads were acquired using a smart cellular phone every 1 min after AEC addition, and the intensity of the T-line was plotted over time (Figure S4, Supporting Information). For the first 5 min, the intensity of T-line increased linearly for all [AEC] examined, so initial rates of the enzymatic reaction were extracted from the slopes these line. Similar to TMB oxidation, reaction kinetics data of AEC oxidation on LFIA strips were fitted well by Michaelis–Menten equation (**Figure 3b**). AEC underwent oxidation by H-Pt NPs immobilized on the T-line, so [H-Pt NP] for AEC oxidation could not be determined; therefore only $K_{m}$ values can be calculated by the equation fitting. The $K_{m}$ value of AEC oxidation by H-Pt NPs on LFIA strip was $1.328 \times 10^{-3}$ M, close to that of TMB oxidation by H-Pt NPs in solution ($1.361 \times 10^{-3}$ M). These results confirm that Ab H-Pt NPs were successfully integrated into LFIA strips to intensify signals by enzyme-like reaction-mediated signal amplification. Finally, we assessed the detection sensitivity of the LFIA strips based on H-Pt NPs. Various [hCG] (0.1–9 ng mL−1) were applied to the sample pads of H-Pt NP-based LFIA strips or to commercially-available Au NP-based LFIA strips. For H-Pt NP-based LFIA strips, sodium acetate buffer ($0.1 \times 10^{-3}$ M, pH 4.5) was applied 5 min after the addition of hCG, to wash away unbound H-Pt NPs and to provide acidic environments in the strips for the peroxidase-like reaction. Then, $3.8 \times 10^{-3}$ M AEC solution in sodium acetate buffer was added to the test pads to amplify the signals; digital images were acquired 5 min after the addition of AEC using a smart cellular phone (top panel of **Figure 4a**). For conventional Au NP-based LFIA strips, digital images of the strips were acquired 15 min after the addition of hCG (bottom panel of **Figure 4a**). The H-Pt NP-based LFIA strips were much more sensitive to hCG than were the Au NP-based LFIA strips: the lowest [hCG] visible by the naked eye was 0.3 ng mL−1 for H-Pt NP-based LFIA strips, but 9 ng mL−1 for Au NP-based LFIA strips. Typically, digital image processing provides better sensitivity than naked-eye examination.[13] Therefore, digital images acquired by a smart cellular phone were processed (Figure S3, Supporting Information) to extract the intensity information of each strip. To compensate for potential variations in intensities depending on acquisition conditions such as light illumination and camera setting, the background was subtracted, and signal was quantified using the ratio (T/C) of the intensity of T-line to the intensity of C-line. T/C of H-Pt NP-based LFIAs and conventional Au NP-based LFIAs increased linearly with [hCG] (**Figure 4b**). The limit of detection (LOD) was defined as [hCG] that corresponds to the T/C value of (average background + 3(standard deviation of background)).[14] LODs were 0.2 ng mL−1 for H-Pt NP-based LFIAs and 3.7 ng mL−1 for Au NP LFIAs. These results agree well with the naked-eye observations. Taken together, H-Pt NP-based LFIA strips were ≈20 times sensitive than conventional Au NP-based LFIA strips. In addition, H-Pt NP-mediated coloration reaction was not altered by biological substances in urine or serum (Figure S5, Supporting Information), meaning H-Pt NP-based LFIAs can be used for real biological samples. In summary, we demonstrated that LFIA strips integrated with H-Pt NPs could detect analytes within 15 min by easy operation and sensitivity ≈20 times higher than that of conventional LFIA strips. In addition, by processing digital images of LFIA strips acquired by a smart cellular phone, which is being ubiquitously available, quantification of analytes could be performed. Considering that H-Pt NPs stored in harsh environments maintained peroxidase-like activity, the H-Pt NP-loaded LFIA strips developed in this study will be useful for many POC bioassay applications including military/biodefense applications[15] and disease diagnostics in tropical countries.[16] **Figure 1.** Schematic illustration of a) conventional and b) enzyme-mimetic NP-based LFIA strips. **Figure 2.** Characterization of H-Pt NPs. a) XRD patterns of H-Pt NPs/Vulcan XC-72. b) SEM image of H-Pt NPs. c,d) HR-TEM images of H-Pt NPs (inset in d: 2.12 Å (111) lattice planes). e,f) peroxidase activity of H-Pt NPs and HRP after 3 h of incubation at a broad range of temperature and pH. **Table 1.** Kinetic parameters of the H-Pt NPs, Pt NPs, and HRP (TMB as a substrate). $K_{m}$: Michaelis–Menten constant; $k_{cat} = V_{max}/[E]$, where $V_{max}$ is the maximal reaction velocity and $[E]$ is the concentration of the enzyme or NPs.

data:
1.  **Experiment 1 (H-Pt NPs in solution):** Identified in Table 1 and Section 3.1. The text explicitly reports kinetic parameters ($K_m$, $k_{cat}$) derived from Michaelis-Menten analysis of TMB oxidation. $K_m$ is $1.361 \times 10^{-3}$ M. $V_{max}$ is not explicitly reported as a standalone value, but $k_{cat}$ ($56.53 \times 10^3$ s$^{-1}$) and $[E]$ ($5.48 \times 10^{-11}$ M) are provided. Per strict "No Calculation" rules, $V_{max}$ is `null` as it is not explicitly stated. Activity is peroxidase. Formula is Pt. Size is 30 nm (TEM).
2.  **Experiment 2 (H-Pt NPs on LFIA strip):** Identified in Section 3.2 and Figure 3b. The text reports a $K_m$ value ($1.328 \times 10^{-3}$ M) for AEC oxidation on the strip. The text explicitly states "$V_{max}$... could not be determined" because the catalyst concentration on the strip was unknown. Thus, $V_{max}$ is `null`. Activity is peroxidase. Formula is Pt. Size is 30 nm (inherited from synthesis).
3.  **Excluded Data:** Pt NPs and HRP data in Table 1 are explicitly noted as "adopted from previous studies" and not part of the current experimental kinetic measurements for the new material, so they are excluded to maintain strict locality to the current study's generated data.

Sweetsop-like α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi catalyst with superior peroxidase-like activity for sensitive and selective detection of hydroquinone†
Min Feng,<sup>a</sup> Shaohua Wen,<sup>a</sup> Xiaofang Chen,<sup>a</sup> Die Deng,<sup>a</sup> Xiupei Yang *<sup>a</sup> and Run Zhang *<sup>b</sup>

Hydroquinone (HQ) is poorly degradable in the ecological environment and is highly toxic to human health even at a low concentration. The colorimetric method has the advantages of low cost and fast analysis, which provides the possibility for simple and rapid detection of HQ. In this work, a new colorimetric method has been developed for HQ detection based on a peroxidase-like catalyst, α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi. This sweetsop-like α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi catalyst enables H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> to produce hydroxyl (•OH), leading to the oxidization of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue oxTMB. In the presence of HQ, the blue oxTMB is reduced to colorless, which allows for colorimetric detection of HQ in water samples. This method has been validated by detecting HQ in water samples with high selectivity, rapid response, broad detection range (0.50 to 30 μM), and low detection limit (0.16 μM).

**Introduction**
Hydroquinone (1,4-dihydroxybenzene, HQ) is a phenolic compound widely used as an industrial reagent in cosmetics, dyes, plastics, textiles, rubber, medicine and other industries.<sup>1–3</sup> During its industrial uses, the unavoidable contamination of the environment, especially water pollution, has threatened aquatic life.<sup>4</sup> This compound is highly toxic to human health even at low concentrations and can cause damage to the skin, mouth, and respiratory system by inhalation.<sup>5–7</sup> HQ is difficult to degrade in the ecological environment, and the accumulation of HQ caused by water discharge has always been a major environmental issue. HQ has been listed as a priority pollutant for aquatic environment monitoring by the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the European Union (EU).<sup>8</sup> Therefore, HQ detection in water samples is essential for environmental regulation and human health. Currently, several methods have been developed for its detection, including electrochemiluminescence (ECL),<sup>9,10</sup> high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC),<sup>11,12</sup> fluorometric method,<sup>13–15</sup> gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (CG-MS),<sup>16</sup> chemiluminescence (CL),<sup>17–19</sup> etc. However, HQ detection by these methods can only be achieved in well-equipped laboratories with the assistance of professional lab technicians, which makes them not suitable for quick and efficient determination of HQ in samples. In recent years, due to the convenience of the naked-eye colorimetric detection method, it is possible to easily and quickly visually inspect samples without any advanced equipment, which has gained considerable attention.<sup>20–22</sup>

Natural enzymes have high substrate specificity and activity and are widely used in medicine, chemical industry, food processing, agriculture, detection, and other fields.<sup>23–25</sup> However, the application of natural enzymes is fundamentally limited by their intrinsic drawbacks.<sup>26,27</sup> Unlike natural enzymes, artificial nanozymes have obvious advantages such as high stability, low cost, and easy scale up.<sup>28,29</sup> Therefore, artificial nanozyme can be an ideal candidate for various enzymatic reactions.<sup>30,31</sup> Since the report of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> nanoparticles' peroxidase activity in 2007, the preparation and application of nanozymes have aroused great interest among researchers.<sup>32</sup> In the past 20 years, a number of nanomaterials have been developed and used as artificial enzymes.<sup>33</sup> These nanomaterials mainly include metal/metal oxide nanoparticles (NPs),<sup>34</sup> metal–organic frameworks (MOFs),<sup>35</sup> carbon nanomaterials,<sup>36</sup> etc. Iron oxide nanoparticles are one of the most typical nanoenzymes. They are widely used in biomedicine, biosensing and other fields due to their unique nanometer properties, stability and enzyme-like activity.<sup>37</sup> As an emerging class of artificial nanozymes, nanomaterials with peroxidase mimetic activity have also been widely used in the development of sensors for the detection of aqueous contaminants.<sup>38,39</sup> Nevertheless, it remains interests to develop artificial nanozymes for rapid and effective colorimetric sensing of organic substrates, such as HQ, because the selection of appropriate catalysts for oxidizing organic substrates to have colour changes is full of challenges.<sup>40</sup>

In this work, we report the development of a new peroxidase-like catalyst, α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi, and its application for colorimetric detection of HQ in water samples. The sweetsop-like α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi was prepared by the one-pot hydrothermal method. The as-synthesized α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi showed high peroxidase activity, stability and was successfully applied to the colorimetric detection of HQ through catalytic oxidation with 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). The experimental results demonstrated that α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi could catalyze H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> to produce •OH which oxidized colorless TMB into blue oxTMB. The blue oxTMB be further reduced to TMB in the presence of HQ. Then a sensor for the colorimetric detection of HQ in water samples has been developed with α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi, H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> and TMB.

**Experimental**

**Materials**
Iron nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO<sub>3</sub>)<sub>3</sub>·9H<sub>2</sub>O), Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO<sub>3</sub>)<sub>2</sub>·6H<sub>2</sub>O), Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid tetrasodium salt hydrate (Na<sub>4</sub>EDTA·xH<sub>2</sub>O), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), hydroquinone (HQ), catechol, resorcinol, phenol, *o*-nitrophenol, *p*-nitrophenol, glucose and other nitrate salts including (Ca<sup>2+</sup>, Cu<sup>2+</sup>, Zn<sup>2+</sup>, Mg<sup>2+</sup>, Co<sup>2+</sup>, Mn<sup>2+</sup>, Cd<sup>2+</sup>, Fe<sup>3+</sup>, Cl<sup>−</sup>, SO<sub>4</sub><sup>2−</sup>, NO<sub>3</sub><sup>−</sup>) were purchased from Aladdin in China. Methanol, hydrogen peroxide (H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, 30%), Acetic acid (C<sub>2</sub>H<sub>4</sub>O<sub>2</sub>), sodium acetate anhydrous (NaAc), nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO<sub>3</sub>)<sub>2</sub>·6H<sub>2</sub>O) were received from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd in China. The tap water and the river were collected from the laboratory and Jialing River of Nanchong, respectively.

**Instrumentation**
Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and mapping detected with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV on JEM-1200EX (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray mappings (EDS) were performed on Hitachi S4800 (Hitachi Limited, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI (Thermo Fisher Scientific, U.S.A.). The X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was measured using a Rigaku D/MAX-2550 (Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation. The infrared spectra were acquired from a Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) with a passed KBr pellet at room temperature. All absorption spectra were detected by a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV-vis absorption spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were recorded using Bruker MS-5000 ESR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany).

**Preparation of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi**
Sweetsop-like α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi were prepared by previous one-pot hydrothermal method reported with some minor modifications.<sup>41</sup> Briefly, a certain amount of EDTA was dissolved in 12 mL ultrapure water to form solution A. A certain amount of Fe(NO<sub>3</sub>)<sub>3</sub>·9H<sub>2</sub>O, Co(NO<sub>3</sub>)<sub>2</sub>·6H<sub>2</sub>O, Ni(NO<sub>3</sub>)<sub>2</sub>·6H<sub>2</sub>O was dissolved in 8 mL ultrapure water to form a uniform mixture solution B. To solution A, solution B was slowly introduced under vigorously stirring. The resulting mixture was allowed to proceed for 10 minutes followed by the addition of 10 mL methanol. The aqueous solution obtained above was transferred to a Teflon-lined container and enclosed into a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave followed by heating at 180 °C for 12 h. After heating, the solution was cooled to room temperature and the solid sample was collected and dried under a vacuum after washing with ultrapure water. The product has a spherical shape with small particles on the surface, and its morphology is remarkably similar to that of sweetsop, so it was named as sweetsop-like α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi. Sweetsop is composed of many round or oval mature carpels connected together, and its aggregate berries are spherical or heart-shaped cones. Other single-metal composites (Co, Ni counterparts) were all fabricated through the same hydrothermal method as sweetsop-like α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi without adding the certain metal ions into solution B.

**Peroxidase-like activity measurements and kinetic studies**
The peroxidase-like catalytic activities of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi nanozymes were investigated by the oxidation of TMB substrate in the presence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. All reactions were carried out in acetate buffer (0.20 M, pH = 6.0), and the absorbance at 652 nm was monitored using a UV-vis absorption spectrophotometer. The reactions were optimized under different α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi concentrations, TMB dosages, H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> dosages, reaction time, temperatures, type of buffer solution, buffer concentration and pH values. Use standard reaction conditions for steady-state kinetic determination, change the concentration of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> (6.0–165.0 mM) at a fixed concentration of TMB (0.35 mM), and *vice versa*, change the concentration of TMB (0.25–2.5 mM) at a fixed concentration of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> (130 mM). The Lineweaver–Burk plots is executed by the double reciprocal of the Michaelis–Menten equation to calculate the Michaelis constant ($K_m$) and the Maximum response speed ($V_{max}$).

**Procedure for visual colorimetric determination of hydroquinone**
Three milliliter of NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 6.0), TMB (0.35 mM), H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> (30%, 40 μL), α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi (3 mg mL<sup>−1</sup>, 30 μL) and the different concentrations of hydroquinone solution were added to a 4 mL spectrophotometer cell. The mixture was further incubated at 20 °C for 14 min followed by monitoring the formation of an oxidation product of TMB at 652 nm using UV-vis spectrophotometry. In order to investigate the selectivity of this colorimetric assay, hydroquinone was replaced by other materials, and the same experimental operations were also conducted. Subsequently, the content of hydroquinone water samples was evaluated according to the same steps described above, of which the samples of hydroquinone were prepared by spiking different concentrations of hydroquinone in real water samples.

**Results and discussion**

**Preparation and characterization of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi nanoparticles**
Sweetsop-like α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi was prepared by the hydrothermal method, and the experimental conditions were optimized with temperatures, reaction times as well as the dosages of nitrate and EDTA (Fig. S1†). Fig. 1 shows the typical SEM image, TEM image, and element mapping images of sweetsop-like α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi. It clearly shows that α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi displays a uniformly spherical shape with a size of about 700 nm. The surface is covered by nanoparticles, like sweetsop (Fig. 1A) and confirmed by the TEM image (Fig. 1B). As shown in Fig. 1C–F, Fe, Co, Ni, and O are homogenously dispersed in nanomaterials, which is consistent with EDS results (Fig. S2†). The marked diffraction peaks of crystalline α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi (Fig. 2A) are in good agreement with the well-established data (JCPDS 33-664). Sweetsop-like α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi, commercial Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> and EDTA were characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy shown in Fig. S3.† It is proved that there are abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi, such as O–H (3400 cm<sup>−1</sup>) and C=O (1600 cm<sup>−1</sup>), which endow them with excellent water dispersibility. The IR peak at 586 cm<sup>−1</sup>, can be attributed to the characteristic stretching vibration of the Fe–O bond.<sup>42</sup> In addition, the absorption bands at 1340 cm<sup>−1</sup> identified the presence of C–N bonds from EDTA. To further confirm the chemical composition and surface state of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi, XPS spectra were used to analyze the surface elemental composition (Fig. 2C–F). The full-range XPS spectrum further confirmed that the elemental components of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi are Fe, Co, Ni, O, and C (Fig. 2C). In the high-resolution spectrum of Fe 2p (Fig. 2D), the two peaks located at 710.5 eV and 724.6 eV correspond to Fe 2p<sub>3/2</sub> and Fe 2p<sub>1/2</sub> respectively, and the energy difference is 14.1 eV, which corresponds to Fe<sup>3+</sup> in α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi. As exhibited in Fig. 2E, two characteristic peaks at 781.0 and 796.9 eV are observed in the Co 2p spectrum, which link to 2p<sub>3/2</sub> and 2p<sub>1/2</sub> doublet of Co<sup>2+</sup> and Co<sup>3+</sup>, respectively. Meanwhile, the Ni<sup>2+</sup> was confirmed by the Ni 2p<sub>3/2</sub> (852.3 eV) and Ni 2p<sub>1/2</sub> (876 eV) peaks in α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi (Fig. 2F).<sup>43</sup> It further guaranteed that the Co and Ni on the surface of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> were successfully immobilized.

**Peroxidase-mimetic activity of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi**
The peroxidase-like activities of the four nanomaterials: commercial Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>, α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@Co, α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@Ni, and α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi were studied by UV-vis absorption spectrum. After incubating α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi with TMB in a buffer solution containing H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> for 14 min, intense absorption bands centered at 652 nm (Fig. S4†) were observed and they were from the oxidation of TMB to oxTMB.<sup>44,45</sup> The changes in solution from colorless to blue suggests the formation of oxTMB. In contrast, negligible changes of absorption spectra were observed after reacting of TMB with commercial Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>, α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@Co and α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@Ni. The UV-vis data indicates that α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi has high catalytic activity for oxidation of TMB. At the same time, Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@Ni has a certain enzymatic activity, and the addition of Co adjusts the electronic structure to a certain extent, increases the electron transfer rate, and further improves its catalytic activity. The synergistic effect of Co, Ni, and α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> may be responsible for such high catalytic activity.

We then investigated the catalytic oxidation ability of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi on the peroxidase substrate TMB to evaluate whether they can be applied as peroxidase mimics. As displayed in Fig. 3, TMB was oxidized to form a blue solution in the presence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> and α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi (inset in Fig. 3). A typical absorption peak of oxTMB centered at 652 nm (cuvette A in Fig. 3) was observed. Changes in UV-vis absorption spectra and solution color were not noticed for other groups, indicating that both H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> and α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi are required for TMB oxidation. Similar to horseradish peroxidase (HRP), α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi greatly enhanced the reaction of TMB with H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, indicating that the α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi can be considered as an efficient peroxidase mimic.

Effects of pH and temperature on α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi-mediated catalytic oxidation were investigated and the results are shown in Fig. S5.† Obviously, the catalytic activity of Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi is dependent on pH and temperature. α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi exhibits high catalytic activity at 20 °C and at pH 6 while others reported at 40–45 °C and at pH 4.5. Other experimental conditions on the catalytic oxidation of TMB in the α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi/H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> system were studied as well including the buffer solution (Fig. S6A and B†), the amount of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi (Fig. S6C†), the dosage of TMB (Fig. S6D†), and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> (Fig. S6E†). The results suggested that the optimal conditions for catalytic oxidation of TMB were HAc-NaAc buffer (3 mL, pH 6), α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi (30 μL, 3 mg mL<sup>−1</sup>), TMB (70 μL, 15 mM), and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> (40 μL, 30%).

**Steady-state kinetics for α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi**
In order to study the peroxidase-like activity of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi, the apparent steady-state kinetic parameters of TMB oxidation were determined by changing the concentrations of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> and TMB (Fig. 4), which is a similar strategy commonly used by HRP enzymes. Within the appropriate concentration range of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> (Fig. 4A and B) and TMB (Fig. 4C and D), a typical Michaelis–Mentenlike curve was achieved. Based on the function of double-reciprocal Lineweaver–Burk plots ($1/V = K_m/V_{max} \times 1/[C] + 1/V_{max}$, where $V_{max}$ is maximum response speed and $K_m$ is an indicator of enzyme affinity toward its substrate. A lower $K_m$ indicates the stronger affinity between enzymes and substrates),<sup>46</sup> Michaelis–Menten constant ($K_m$) was 0.23, 0.42 mM for TMB and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, respectively, and maximum initial velocity ($V_{max}$) was $13.5 \times 10^{−8}$ and $9.3 \times 10^{−8}$ M s<sup>−1</sup> for TMB and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, respectively (Table S1†). As can be seen from the table, α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi possesses smaller $K_m$ and higher $V_{max}$ than other nanozymes, indicating its outstanding peroxidase-like activity.

**Selective and sensitive colorimetric detection of HQ**
Fig. 5A and B show the time-dependent scanning kinetic curve and UV-Vis spectra of the α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi in TMB/H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> system at different HQ dosages. The absorbance at 652 nm decreased significantly with the increasing HQ concentration, indicating that HQ successfully inhibited the oxidation of TMB. As shown in Fig. 5C, UV-vis absorption of the solution containing TMB, H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, and α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi were gradually reduced upon the addition of HQ. A good linear relationship ($R^2 = 0.996$) was obtained when plotting the absorbance at 652 nm against the HQ concentration range of 0.50–30 μM. The detection limit 0.16 μM (0.018 mg L<sup>−1</sup>) is lower than the U.S. Environment Protection Agency estimated wastewater discharge limit of 0.5 mg L<sup>−1</sup>. Compared with other methods (Table S2†), our approach has a higher sensitivity for HQ detection. The selective response of the proposed method for HQ detection over other ions and molecules was evaluated in the aqueous solutions containing TMB, H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> and α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi, 20-fold of potential interference species, including Fe<sup>3+</sup>, Cd<sup>2+</sup>, Ca<sup>2+</sup>, Mn<sup>2+</sup>, Mg<sup>2+</sup>, Cu<sup>2+</sup>, Co<sup>2+</sup>, Zn<sup>2+</sup>, Cl<sup>−</sup>, SO<sub>4</sub><sup>2−</sup>, NO<sub>3</sub><sup>−</sup>, glucose, catechol, resorcinol, phenol, *o*-nitrophenol, *p*-nitrophenol, and glycine. Changes of the UV-vis absorbance ($(A_0 − A)/A_0$) were determined after 1 min incubation and presented in Fig. 6. As shown in Fig. 6, no significant changes in $(A_0 − A)/A_0$ were observed upon the addition of ions and molecules except HQ. Catechol has a similar structure to hydroquinone, which might interfere the HQ detection (Fig. S7†). The interference from catechol may be minimized by using activated alumina as it has been reported that activated alumina can adsorb catechol. The optimum amount of alumina used was explored to not interfere with the detection of HQ while adsorbing catechol and the results are shown in Fig. S8.†

**Further understanding of the detection mechanism**
According to previous reports, the catalytic mechanism of nanozymes can be divided into two categories: one is the transfer of electrons between the substrate and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, and the other is the active substance in the catalytic system.<sup>38,47</sup> In order to study the catalytic mechanism, isopropanol (IPA) was added to the solution containing TMB, H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, and α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi. As a hydroxyl radical scavenger, IPA was often used to detect hydroxyl radicals in the oxidation process.<sup>48</sup> As shown in Fig. S9,† the absorption at 652 nm is significantly reduced after adding IPA (10 mM), which proves the generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH). At the same time, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy is used to explore the reaction mechanism and the active free radicals generated during the reaction. As shown in Fig. 2B, a characteristic signal peak of DMPO-HO• with an intensity of 1 : 2 : 2 : 1 was generated after the addition of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi, indicating that α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi can catalyze H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> to produce •OH.<sup>49</sup> Based on the experimental results and previous reports, the mechanism of the peroxide-like activity of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi was proposed and illustrated in Fig. 7. H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> is first adsorbed on the surface of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi, and then α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi promotes the decomposition of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> to produce •OH, which can further oxidize TMB and produce blue substances. The addition of HQ reduces oxTMB to TMB, which is oxidized to *p*-benzoquinone.

**Determination of HQ in real samples**
To validate the feasibility of the proposed method for HQ colorimetric detection in tap water and river water, HQ was spiked into them, and the concentration of HQ was measured thereafter. As shown in Table 1, the recoveries of HQ in spiked samples were in the range of 91.3–102.8%, with RSDs <5%. Such results demonstrated that the proposed method has good accuracy and precision for HQ detection in spiked water samples.

**Conclusions**
Sweetsop-like α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi prepared by hydrothermal method was characterized by SEM, TEM, XRD, and XPS. An efficient and convenient HQ quantitative detection method was developed based on the peroxidase-like activity of α-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>@CoNi. This developed colorimetric method for HQ detection exhibited high sensitivity (detection limit 0.16 μM) and selectivity and was validated *via* spiked water samples.
